raw_case:
The Subtle evolution of pulmonary parenchymal changes: A case of lady windermere syndrome emerging from chronic MAC infection in an immunocompetent patient with scoliosis
Mycobacterium avium complex, a commonly seen infection in individuals with a compromised immune system, can also affect people who are immunocompetent. One particularly important manifestation is in elderly females with a competent immune system, involving the middle lobe and lingula lobe of the lung. This is known as Lady Windermere syndrome (LWS). An 84-year-old female patient with certain nonpulmonary comorbidities initially got a diagnosis of MAC infection but refused to undergo any medication therapy, she presents after 5 years for a follow up visit and is found to have significant changes in her pulmonary parenchyma on investigation findings, concerning for this syndrome. LWS is a rather uncommon condition, rarely presents in clinical settings and described very less often in the literature. This report is aimed at increasing awareness of LWS as a possible diagnosis that should be considered in patients with a chronic MAC infection and a specific pattern of parenchymal changes discussed in this report in detail.

Introduction

In 1992, Reich and Johnson first explained Lady Windermere syndrome. Six cases of female patients, all of them old in age, were reported in which right middle lobe of the lung was involved [1]. Patients affected with this condition were found to suppress their cough voluntarily, which has been suggested to play a role in developing [2] a pattern consisting of cylindrical bronchiectasis involving bronchiectasis of right middle lobe or lingular segment and reticulonodular infiltrates due to an infection of Mycobacterium avium intracellulare.MAC was classically known to affect people with compromised immune systems and the ones with underlying pulmonary parenchymal diseases. The presence of chronic MAC infection in people without any underlying lung disease or immunocompromised state led to a search for other possible causes. A similar pattern of presentation in patients with matching exposure and morbidity status led to recognition of this syndrome as a possible sequelae of chronic MAC infection [3].

Case presentation

An 82-year-old lady, nonsmoker, tall, slender, thin built with body mass index (BMI) of 21.24 kg/m2, has scoliosis, socially active who is a known case of pulmonary Mycobacterium Avium Complex (MAC) presented to the clinic for follow-up after a computed tomography scan of chest (CT chest) and pulmonary function tests. 5 years ago, she was seen in the clinic for a mild chronic dry cough.At that time, pulmonary function tests were normal shown in (Table 1.1, Table 1.2). CT scan (Fig. 1) showed biapical pleural parenchymal scarring, clustered nodules in the right upper lobe laterally as well as anteriorly, mild traction bronchiectasis in the right upper lobe, lingula and right middle lobe with nodules likely representing mucous plugging, 5 mm nodule in the right lower and Arteriovenous malformation (AVM) in right middle lobe and Bronchoscopy showed Right lower lung (RLL) and lingula pulmonary nodules. The bronchoalveolar lavage (BAL) culture was positive for MAC and negative for acid fast bacilli, fungus and viruses. MAC susceptibility Broth showed susceptibility for amikacin, clarithromycin and resistance to linezolid and Moxifloxacin. The cytology report showed no malignant cells. Due to side effects of the treatment, the patient did not want to start on the medications for the MAC.On this follow-up visit for pulmonary function tests and CT scan of chest, patient suppresses any cough, sputum production is minimal, shortness of breath is close to her baseline, and she is quite active in her activities. Of note, she has multiple comorbidities, including arthritis, acid reflux, hypertension, osteoporosis, and stroke.Physical examination revealed a thin woman with a BMI of 21.24kg/m2. She had scoliosis and no chest wall deformity. Trachea and pulmonary efforts were normal. Overall, she is doing well. Examination of the right and left lower lung fields revealed decreased breath sounds and no wheezing, rhonchi or rales were heard.Her pulmonary function tests showed overall mild obstruction, flow-volume loop showed mild obstructive pattern, absent bronchodilator response and normal diffusion capacity (Table 2.1, Table 2.2). Chest X-ray showed scoliosis and bilateral hilar nodules as given in the Fig. 2. CT chest without contrast showed overall scattered areas of cylindrical and saccular bronchiectasis with pulmonary nodularity and right lobe tree-in-bud configuration. CT chest also showed a small hiatal hernia. Overall, the appearance is consistent with chronic mycobacterium avium infection (Fig. 3).Given the above findings, a diagnosis of Lady Windermere syndrome was made. Due to side effects of the medication and absence of the typical concerning symptoms of worsening of pulmonary MAC she is not started on medications and follow-up will be done in close to 1 year time. PFT at the follow up visit was requested.

Discussion

Mycobacteria are gram-positive, acid-fast bacilli. They are the causative agents behind tuberculosis and leprosy. There are more than 190 species, and only two of them are tuberculous types which cause the abovementioned diseases [4]. All other species are nontubercular types, known as nontubercular mycobacteria (NTM). Based on Runyon classification, there are 4 different groups of NTM, classified on the basis of their growth rates, pigmentation and morphology. According to this classification system, MAC is placed in group 3.Pulmonary disease, lymphadenitis, cutaneous disease, and disseminated disease are the main clinical symptoms brought on by NTM [5]. NTM infections are typically associated with immunocompromised people, although they can also occasionally affect immunocompetent patients. When they do, there is a 77% chance of a resulting pulmonary illness [6].Pulmonary disease caused by MAC infection affects the upper lobe of the lungs. It can cause cavitation or nodular bronchiectasis. The nodular bronchiectatic form, also known as LWS, generally occurs in patients with no history of lung conditions, whereas the upper lobe cavitary form typically affects patients with underlying lung conditions such as COPD, silicosis, and a history of tuberculosis infection.Lady Windermere is a character in a Victorian-era play written by Oscar Wilde. The main character behaved in an uncompromising manner [7], in which the urge to cough was suppressed in order to avoid looking ill-mannered. Thin older women with underlying bronchiectasis are found to have a nodular bronchiectatic illness, which is the form of MAC infection associated with the syndromic name. It has been proposed that suppression of cough allows secretions to collect in the airways, especially in the middle lobe and lingula, forming a nidus for inflammation and chronic mycobacterial infection.The diagnosis of LWS can be made through certain specific radiological findings. Two major tools include chest X-ray and CT scan. Chest X-ray findings include the middle lobe and lingular linear opacities while the CT scan images can show bronchiectatic pattern, centrilobular nodules, tree-in-bud nodules affecting the middle lobe and lingula [8].In our patient, the initial CT scan findings, 5 years ago were characterized biapical pleural parenchymal scarring, clustered nodules in the right upper lobe laterally as well as anteriorly, mild traction bronchiectasis in the right upper lobe, lingula and right middle lobe with nodules likely representing mucous plugging, 5 mm nodule in the right lower lobe and Arteriovenous malformation (AVM) in right middle lobe. The findings of the follow up CT scan include scattered areas of cylindrical and saccular bronchiectasis with pulmonary nodularity and right lobe tree-in-bud configuration. The overall appearance is consistent with chronic mycobacterium avium infectionChronic MAC infection is treated with a combination of Macrolides (Azithromycin or Clarithromycin), Ethambutol, and Rifampicin [9]. Surgical interventions can be considered in cases where medical management fails. Surgical intervention such as selective pulmonary resection [10] can be attempted in a small group of patients.

Conclusion

In conclusion, our patient who is a known case of MAC infection, presented for a follow up 5 years after the initial visit in which MAC infection was diagnosed. At that time, this patient with scoliosis had normal pulmonary function tests and CT scan findings along with BAL confirmed MAC infection, but the patient did not want to receive pharmacologic treatment due to the expected side effects. Now, at 5 years follow up, there is derangement of pulmonary function tests, and the CT scan findings now show honeycombing of the pulmonary parenchyma which consistent with chronic MAC infection. The disease courses and the relevant investigation findings indicate that this is a case of Lady Windermere Syndrome.

Patient consent

Written informed consent was obtained from the patient for publication of this case report and accompanying images. A copy of the written consent is available for review by the Editor-in-Chief of this journal on request.

generate_case.case_summary:
- **Patient Information:** 82-year-old female, nonsmoker, slender with BMI of 21.24 kg/m².
- **Chief Complaint:** None reported at follow-up visit.
- **History of Present Illness:** Diagnosed with pulmonary Mycobacterium avium complex (MAC) infection five years ago but refused pharmacologic treatment due to concerns about side effects. At that time, presented with mild chronic dry cough. Now presents for follow-up evaluation. Reports no worsening of symptoms such as persistent cough, sputum production, or shortness of breath. 
- **Past Medical History:** Scoliosis, arthritis, hypertension, osteoporosis, acid reflux, and stroke.
- **Personal History:** Socially active.
- **Family History:** None reported.
- **Physical Examination:** Thin woman with scoliosis, no chest wall deformities, no wheezing, rhonchi, or rales on lung auscultation. Decreased breath sounds noted in bilateral lower lung fields.
- **Ancillary Tests:**
  - Pulmonary function tests (PFTs): Showed mild obstruction and absent bronchodilator response with normal diffusion capacity.
  - Chest X-ray: Revealed scoliosis and bilateral hilar nodules.
  - CT chest: Scattered cylindrical and saccular bronchiectasis, pulmonary nodularity, tree-in-bud pattern in the right lobe, and a small hiatal hernia. Findings consistent with chronic MAC infection.
  - Bronchoscopy (5 years ago): Confirmatory bronchoalveolar lavage (BAL) culture positive for MAC with susceptibility to amikacin and clarithromycin, resistance to linezolid and moxifloxacin. Negative for malignant cells, acid-fast bacilli, fungi, or viruses.

---
generate_case.differential_diagnosis:
1. **Pulmonary MAC Infection (Primary Consideration):**
   - Patient's 5-year-long course of chronic MAC infection fits findings of bronchiectasis and nodularity, especially in the middle lobe and lingula.
   - CT patterns of cylindrical and saccular bronchiectasis with tree-in-bud configuration are hallmark features of chronic MAC infection.
   - No other systemic or immunocompromised condition to suggest an alternate cause like disseminated MAC.

2. **Lady Windermere Syndrome (LWS):**
   - LWS is a specific manifestation of chronic MAC infection in immunocompetent individuals, typically elderly thin women.
   - Radiological findings, history of suppressed cough, and absence of other lung conditions like COPD or prior tuberculosis support this diagnosis.
   - Consistent with patient phenotype (thin, elderly female with scoliosis).

3. **Exclusion of Other Nontuberculous Mycobacterial (NTM) Infections:**
   - MAC confirmed via BAL culture, ruling out other NTM pathogens as the causative agent.

4. **Exclusion of Malignancy:**
   - Previous cytology findings negative for malignant cells, suggesting infectious etiology rather than neoplasia.
    
Summary: With a consistent clinical pattern and corroborative radiological findings, Lady Windermere Syndrome secondary to chronic MAC infection remains the primary diagnosis. Differential diagnoses such as malignancies, systemic NTM disease, or other lung disease were effectively ruled out.

---
generate_case.final_diagnosis:
**Lady Windermere Syndrome (LWS)** secondary to chronic Mycobacterium avium complex (MAC) infection in an immunocompetent host with scoliosis, presenting with chronic changes in the pulmonary parenchyma and bronchiectasis primarily involving the middle lobe and lingula.

---

results.thinking_process:
Okay, let me start by going through the patient's information again. She's an 82-year-old woman with a history of pulmonary MAC infection diagnosed five years ago. She didn't take the recommended medications because she was worried about side effects. Now, she's here for a follow-up and hasn't reported any new symptoms. Her main complaints five years ago were a mild dry cough, and now she's saying that her symptoms like cough, sputum, or shortness of breath haven't gotten worse. 

Looking at her past medical history, she has scoliosis, arthritis, hypertension, osteoporosis, acid reflux, and a history of stroke. No family history mentioned. Her physical exam shows she's thin with scoliosis, decreased breath sounds in the lower lungs but no other abnormal lung sounds. The ancillary tests are key here. Her PFTs show mild obstruction without bronchodilator response, which might suggest a fixed airway issue, not asthma. The chest X-ray shows bilateral hilar nodules and scoliosis. The CT chest findings are significant: bronchiectasis (both cylindrical and saccular), nodularity, tree-in-bud pattern in the right lobe. The bronchoscopy from five years ago confirmed MAC with certain susceptibilities. 

So, she was diagnosed with MAC five years ago but didn't treat it. The current CT still shows changes consistent with chronic MAC infection. The tree-in-bud pattern is classic for infections like MAC, especially in bronchiectasis. The absence of worsening symptoms is interesting. But the imaging hasn't resolved, and since she didn't receive treatment, the infection is likely still present. Even though she's stable symptomatically, the CT findings and previous positive culture point to ongoing chronic MAC. 

Other possibilities to consider: Could there be another infection or a non-infectious process? The negative cultures for other pathogens five years ago and the stability of symptoms make other infections less likely. The bronchiectasis might be due to MAC itself, as it's a known cause. Her scoliosis might contribute to some of the decreased breath sounds but doesn't explain the nodules or tree-in-bud. Hiatal hernia is noted but unrelated here. 

The diagnostic criteria for MAC pulmonary disease typically require clinical symptoms, radiographic findings, and positive cultures. She had all three five years ago. Since she didn't get treated, the infection is likely persisting. Even without symptom progression, the chronic nature and imaging support the diagnosis. 

So putting it all together, the final diagnosis would be chronic pulmonary MAC infection.
results.content:
```
### Resoning:
<step 1> The patient has a confirmed history of pulmonary MAC infection diagnosed via bronchoscopy five years ago, with CT findings (bronchiectasis, tree-in-bud pattern, nodularity) consistent with chronic MAC. Despite refusing treatment, her symptoms (dry cough) remain stable without progression, suggesting indolent or non-progressive disease.

<step 2> The absence of new or worsening respiratory symptoms (e.g., sputum production, dyspnea, hemoptysis) and stable imaging findings over five years argue against acute exacerbation or progression to fibrocavitary MAC disease. Chronic MAC infections often exhibit slow progression or stability in milder nodular/bronchiectatic forms, particularly in older, nonsmoking females with structural lung changes.

<step 3> The persistent bronchiectasis and tree-in-bud patterns on CT, unchanged from prior imaging, confirm ongoing structural damage typical of chronic MAC infection. Normal diffusion capacity and stable PFTs (mild obstruction) further support non-progressive airway involvement without significant functional decline.

<step 4> Alternative diagnoses (e.g., tuberculosis, fungal infection, malignancy) are excluded by prior negative BAL cultures for other pathogens, lack of malignant cells, and absence of new concerning radiographic features. Hiatal hernia and scoliosis are incidental and unrelated to the pulmonary findings.

### Answer:
Chronic pulmonary Mycobacterium avium complex (MAC) infection (nodular/bronchiectatic form).
